coredata.dc:title: In-situ hydrothermal synthesis of molecularly imprinted polymers coated carbon dots for fluorescent detection of bisphenol A
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2016 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Guoliang
Chen, Zhuo
Jiang, Xiaoying
Feng, Da-Qian
Zhao, Junyu
Fan, Dahe
Wang, Wei
coredata.dc:description: A new fluorescent approach for bisphenol A (BPA) was first developed based on eco-friendly molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) coated carbon dots (CDs) composite. Fluorescent carbon dots were synthesized by hydrothermal synthesis, and their surface was encapsulated in the pores of silica of MIP matrix (MIP-coated CDs) in situ. The MIP-coated CDs composite exhibited strong fluorescent property and high selectivity as it incorporated both merits of CDs and MIP. After the removal of original template molecules, the nanocomposite displayed quite high selectivity for BPA. As the concentration of BPA increased, the fluorescent signal gradually decreased. The fluorescent response for BPA detection was in the linear ranges from 100nM to 4200nM with the detection limit of 30nM (S/N =3). The proposed method was successfully applied to the detection of BPA in river water samples with satisfactory results. Moreover, the strategy of MIP-coated CDs composite as a novel fluorescent biosensor paves a new way to improve the performance for fluorescent sensors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Molecularly imprinted polymers
In-situ hydrothermal synthesis
Bisphenol A
Fluorescent sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Measure of BPA in real samples
Characterization of the as-prepared MIP-coated CDs
Extraction and adsorption of the template in the MIP-coated CDs
Detection of BPA based on MIP-coated CDs
Detection of BPA in real river water samples

Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustration of the preparation of MIP-coated CDs.
(a) UV–vis absorption spectrum (left line) and fluorescence emission spectrum (right line) of pre-prepared CDs. (b) FTIR spectra of AEAPMS (up line) and prepared CDs (down line).
(a) TEM image of synthesized MIP-coated CDs. (b) UV–vis absorption (left line) and fluorescence emission (right line) of fabricated MIP-coated CDs.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of fabricated NIP-coated CDs (1), MIP-coated CDs before (2) and after (3) the template molecules extraction, MIP-coated CDs (50mg/mL) with the addition of 3μM BPA solution (4). (b) FTIR spectra of MIP-coated CDs before (1) and after (2) the template molecules extraction, and NIP-coated CDs (3).

Fluorescence emission spectra of the MIP-coated CDs (a) and NIP-coated CDs (b) system after the introduction of different concentrations of BPA. Conditions: 1, MIP-coated or NIP-coated CDs; 2–12: 1+ C BPA (μM): 0.1, 0.4, 0.8, 1.2, 1.8, 2.4, 3.0, 3.6, 4.2, 4.8, and 5.4.

(a) Comparison the calibration curves of BPA concentration of MIP-coated with that of NIP-coated CDs. The error bars= ±standard deviation. (b) The cycle curves of the prepared MIP-coated CDs in the absence (the up dot) and presence (the bottom dot) of 4.8μM BPA.

The MIP-coated CDs based system toward various potential interfering substances. Conditions: The concentration of MIP-coated CDs is 1mg/mL. The concentrations of various ions including K+, Na+, Ca2+, Zn2+, CO3 2−, SO4 2−, Fe3+, Ag+, Pb2+, Hg2+, and Cu2+ are 10, 10, 10, 10, 10, 10, 5, 5, 5, 3, and 3μM, respectively. The concentrations of phenol, o-nitrophenol (o-NP), paranitrophenol (4-NP) and BPA are 2.4μM. F 0 and F are fluorescent intensities of the C-dots (CDs) before and after adding the corresponding interfering substances, respectively.
Comparison of performance with different methods.
Results of the determination of BPA in river water samples by MIP coated CDs based sensor and HPLC.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Average values are from six independent measurements at the same conditions.

head.ce:title: In-situ hydrothermal synthesis of molecularly imprinted polymers coated carbon dots for fluorescent detection of bisphenol A

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Yancheng Institute of Technology, Yancheng 224051, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new fluorescent approach for bisphenol A (BPA) was first developed based on eco-friendly molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) coated carbon dots (CDs) composite. Fluorescent carbon dots were synthesized by hydrothermal synthesis, and their surface was encapsulated in the pores of silica of MIP matrix (MIP-coated CDs) in situ. The MIP-coated CDs composite exhibited strong fluorescent property and high selectivity as it incorporated both merits of CDs and MIP. After the removal of original template molecules, the nanocomposite displayed quite high selectivity for BPA. As the concentration of BPA increased, the fluorescent signal gradually decreased. The fluorescent response for BPA detection was in the linear ranges from 100nM to 4200nM with the detection limit of 30nM (S/N =3). The proposed method was successfully applied to the detection of BPA in river water samples with satisfactory results. Moreover, the strategy of MIP-coated CDs composite as a novel fluorescent biosensor paves a new way to improve the performance for fluorescent sensors.

ce:section.ce:para: The molecular imprinting technique (MIT) is one of the most promising techniques in sensor field due to its simplicity, reliability, and low cost [1,2]. The molecular imprinting is a technique for the synthesis of artificial molecular imprinting polymer (MIP) under the guidance of template molecule to fabricate complementary binding sites with specific recognition ability [3]. Among various methods for the preparation of MIP, sol–gel polymerization has attracted more attentions because of its distinct advantages such as easy fabrication, eco-friendly synthesis, and mild polymerization conditions [4].

Bisphenol A (BPA), one of important synthetic materials, lies in widely food-storage or packaging materials such as nursing bottles, beverage container, dinner ware and microwave ovenware [5]. BPA is an environmental endocrine disrupting chemical (EDC) that can bind to the estrogenic receptors and induce the proliferation of them [6]. The study suggests that increasing amount of BPA in the adult urine is positively associated with cardiovascular disease, diabetes and impaired reproductive capacity [7,8]. It has been reported that low doses of BPA, even sub-ng levels (0.23ng/L), can also affect human health [9]. In recent years, the amount of bisphenol A increases gradually in the river water due to the random discard of plastic garbage [10]. Thus, it is quite significant to develop the new method for highly sensitive and highly selective determining BPA. So far, different methods have been reported for detecting BPA such as chromatographic techniques [11,12], electrochemical detection [13,14], chemiluminescent detection [15] and fluorescent detection [16]. Among them, fluorescent method or fluorimetry is the most popular method as for its unique merits, for example, simple, reliable, and low cost.

Several optical sensors have been constructed on the surface of semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) coated with molecularly imprinted silica film through the sol–gel polymerization approach [17,18]. These prepared materials display special binding and selective recognition for the template molecules. However, these methods need the complicated pretreatment. QDs have to be capped with organosilane before the sol–gel hydrolysis and condensation imprinted polymerization reaction, which improves the interaction between QDs and molecularly imprinted silica matrix. Furthermore, heavy-metal-based QDs have toxicological effect on humans and the environment. Therefore, it is extremely important to explore a facile and eco-friendly new fluorescent method.

Carbon dots (CDs), as a new fluorescent nanomaterial, have been drawn an increasing attention due to their attractive applications in biomedical imaging [19,20]. Compared with conventional organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots, fluorescent CDs are superior in terms of chemical inertness, tunable photoluminescence property, excellent biocompatibility, and low toxicity [21,22]. To apply CDs in the sensor field, it is desirable to embed them in an appropriate solid matrix or prepare in solid-state architectures, which can remain fluorescent properties of CDs. Silica gel and organically modified silicates are ideal materials owing to their inherent stability [23,24].

In this contribution, a facile and label-free new fluorescent approach of sensing bisphenol A was proposed based on MIP-coated CDs composite. CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method. After that, CDs were capsulated with silica imprinted film through one-step sol–gel reaction in situ (Scheme 1 ). The fluorescence properties of CDs are remained as silica is an inert material [25]. In our design strategy, fluorescent CDs act as the signal component while imprinted silica film serves as the recognition element. The new fluorescent method based on MIP-coated CDs composite incorporates excellent fluorescent property of CDs and high selectivity of imprinted silica materials. Compared with traditional methods, the presented approach has outstanding performances such as facile synthesis, label-free, high selectivity and sensitivity. Moreover, the developed assay opens up new avenues for MIP-coated CDs composite applied in fluorescent sensor.

Bisphenol A (BPA), N-(β-aminoethyl)-γ-aminopropylmethyldimethoxysila-ne (AEAPMS), TEOS (tetraethoxysilane), APTES (3-aminopropyl-triethoxy-silane), citric acid anhydrous were purchased from Aladdin Chemical Co., Ltd. (USA). Ammonia solution (25% in water), petroleum ether, and ethanol were bought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All reagents were of analytical grade, and used without further purification. Millipore Milli-Q ultrapure water (18.2MΩcm) was used throughout the whole research.

The UV–vis absorption spectra were carried out on a Shimadzu 245 UV–vis spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The fluorescence measurements were carried out on Jasco FP-6500 fluorescence spectrometer (Jasco, Japan). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopic measurements were performed on a NEXUS-670 Fourier transform spectrometer (Nicolet, USA). The high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images were observed by a Model JEM 2010 electron microscope (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan).

In a typical experiment, CDs were prepared according to the previously reported method with a little modification [26]. Briefly, 0.5g citric acid anhydrous is dissolved in 10mL N-(β-aminoethyl)-γ-aminopropylmethyldimethoxysilane (AEAPMS) and loaded into a 50mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, and degassed with nitrogen for about 20min. Then, the autoclave was kept at 240°C for 2h. After the reaction, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature. The solution was filtered with cylinder filtration membrane filter (0.22μm). The final products were washed with petroleum ether three times. The obtained CDs were dispersed in a certain amount of ethanol and stored in the refrigerator at 4°C for further use.

The imprinting polymer was fabricated on silica-coated CDs via a typical sol–gel polymerization as previously reported with some modifications [27] (Scheme 1). 200mg BPA and 40mL CDs anhydrous ethanol solution were added to a 100mL beaker by using ultrasound to assist BPA dissolution. Then, 350μL APTES (functional monomer), 1.5mL TEOS (cross-linker), 200μL ammonia solution and 800μL ultrapure water were injected to the beaker with stirring. Finally, the mixture was performed to proceed for 24h at room temperature in dark. The resulting product was collected by centrifugation and washed thoroughly with ethanol to discard the reagents. Original BPA templates in the synthesized MIP-coated CDs composite were extracted with ethanol and water (v/v=1:1), until no template was detected from the washing solvent with UV spectrometer. Meanwhile, the non-imprinted NIP-coated CDs composite were prepared using the same method without adding any template molecules. The two kinds of polymer materials were dried in a vacuum chamber at 60°C overnight. The obtained MIP-coated CDs composite was dissolved in 8mL water under ultrasonication and kept refrigerated prior to use.

The obtained oven dried powder of MIP-coated CDs was dissolved into ultrapure water to make MIP-coated CDs working solution (100μg/mL). 100μL working solution was added to the cuvette, and then varies concentrations of BPA solutions were introduced. The fluorescent measurements were performed with the excitation wavelength of 360nm. The fluorescence spectrum was measured immediately after adequately mixing. The control experiments of NIP-coated CDs were the same as those of MIP-coated CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measure of BPA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The real surface river water samples were collected from local Tongyu River (Yancheng, Jiangsu Province, China). The samples were filtered through 0.45μm microporous membrane to eliminate particulate matter and stored in clean glass bottles. As no obvious BPA in the collected water samples was detected by the proposed assay, a recovery study was carried out for the determination of the analyte by the standard addition method.

As shown in Scheme 1, the synthesis produce of CDs involved decomposition and pyrolysis of anhydrous citric acid, meanwhile the acylation reaction surface passivation occurred simultaneously in the presence of AEAPMS. To evaluate the optical properties of prepared CDs, UV–vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectrum were carried out (Fig. 1 a). There was an absorption peak located at 360nm in the UV–vis absorption spectrum (black line). The fluorescence spectrum of synthesized CDs exhibited a maximum emission peak at 460nm (blue line) under the excitation of 360nm. The quantum yield of the prepared CDs was 22% by using quinine sulfate as the standard. The above synthesized procedure was confirmed by FTIR spectroscopy (Fig. 1b). Compared with that of AEAPMS, the FTIR spectrum of prepared CDs displayed three different absorption peaks. The peak at 1565cm−1 assigns to secondary amide NH bending and CN stretching while the peak at 1650cm−1 assigns to secondary amide CO stretching, and the broad peak at 3291cm−1 assigns to secondary amide NH stretching. These situations revealed the formation of amide bonding (CONH), which was the most characteristic features that indicated the successful surface passivation reaction of CDs. Those situations suggest that CDs were successfully synthesized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of the as-prepared MIP-coated CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Template molecules BPA interacted with APTES through the hydrogen bond interactions and van der Waals forces, and further copolymerized with surface functionalized CDs under the catalysis of aqueous ammonia. Consequently, the template molecules were successfully embedded in the silica-coated matrix. These phenomena corresponded with the copolymerization of APTES and TEOS on the surface of silica-coated CDs [28]. After the redundant reagents and template molecules were removed by washing with anhydrous ethanol and distilled water, the MIP-coated CDs nanocomposites with specific BPA recognition sites were obtained (Scheme 1).

The morphology of MIP-coated CDs nanocomposites was characterized by transmission electron microscope (TEM). The image of MIP-coated CDs particles showed spherical nanostructure with approximately 50nm diameter (Fig. 2 a). Meanwhile, average size of silica-coated CDs was 25nm (Fig. S1, Supplementary material). The comparison demonstrated the successful fabrication of MIP-coated CDs. As displayed in Fig. 2b, MIP-coated CDs did not have characteristic absorption peak in the range of 250–650nm, which suggested that it successfully removed the template molecules in the MIP-coated CDs nanocomposites. Besides, the prepared MIP-coated CDs indicated a symmetric fluorescent emission peak at 460nm when excited at 360nm (Fig. S2, Supplementary material), which revealed that the silica coating did not restrict the fluorescence properties of synthesized CDs as the silica is optically insert.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Extraction and adsorption of the template in the MIP-coated CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The NIP-coated CDs nanocomposites show strong fluorescence signal (curve 1 of Fig. 3 a) while MIP-coated CDs nanocomposites with template molecules display weak fluorescent signal (curve 2 of Fig. 3a). The template molecules were extracted through the conventional solvent elution method. As shown in curve 3 of Fig. 3a, after the template extraction, the fluorescence intensity of MIP-coated CDs nanocomposites nearly restored to that of NIP-coated CDs. However, the fluorescence of MIP-coated CDs without the template molecules quenched a lot after the introduction of BPA molecules (curve 4 of Fig. 3a). This phenomenon originated from the charge transfer between CDs and BPA molecules, which was responsible for the fluorescence quenching of the MIP-coated CDs. In the recognition system, the CDs and BPA molecules played as electron donor and electron acceptor, respectively. Thus, the fluorescence of MIP-coated CDs was effectively quenched after the interaction with BPA molecules.

To ascertain the successful imprinting template molecule BPA into the silica matrix, FTIR spectra of NIP-coated CDs and MIP-coated CDs before and after template molecules extraction were performed. As shown in curve 1 of Fig. 3b, MIP-coated CDs has a characteristic peak at 2940cm−1 assigning to the CH stretching vibration while NIP-coated CDs has this characteristic peak at 2974cm−1. The phenomenon of approximate 30nm displacement attributed to the influence of hydrogen bonding force on BPA molecularly imprinted materials. In other words, the presence of hydrogen bonding force in materials resulted in blue-shift of characteristic peaks. The characteristic peak at 1496cm−1 represented the aromatic ring stretching vibration of BPA molecule [29]. After the solvent elution, this characteristic peak disappeared in the FTIR spectrum of MIP-coated CDs (Fig. 3b). These experimental results revealed that BPA molecules had been successfully imprinted into the silica matrix, and effectively washed out during the solvent extraction process. The FTIR peaks around 1062cm−1 and 794cm−1 represent to the SiOSi asymmetric stretching and SiO vibrations, respectively. All those bands further suggested that the MIP generated from sol–gel condensation was successfully grafted on the surface of the silicon-coated CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of BPA based on MIP-coated CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
To demonstrate the recognition ability of the MIP-coated CDs, the fluorescence spectral response of MIP-coated CDs after the introduction of varies concentrations of BPA solution was measured. As displayed in Fig. 4 a and b, compared with that of the NIP-coated CDs, the fluorescence intensity of the MIP-coated CDs decreased more sensitively after the addition of the same concentration of BPA solution. This phenomenon contributed to the interaction between MIP matrix and BPA molecule via hydrogen bonds and van der Wall forces. Moreover, the response time of the MIP-coated CDs and BPA was investigated. The results display that the reaction process reaches the balance at no more than one minute. In order to investigate the interaction between CDs and BPA, series of BPA solutions at different concentrations were prepared. The fluorescence intensity of prepared CDs reduced gradually with the increasing BPA concentration. This process accorded with the Stern–Volmer equation [30].
F
0
F
= 1 +
K
s y
×
C
B P A
where F 0 and F are the fluorescent intensities in the absence and presence of BPA, respectively, K sv is the Stern–Volmer constant, and C BPA is the added concentration of BPA. As illustrated in Fig. 5 a, the ratio of the K MIP and K NIP (K MIP/K NIP) which are the linear slopes is defined as imprinting factor (IF), which is usually selected to evaluate the selectivity of the materials [31]. Upon optimum conditions, the IF (K MIP/K NIP) of the detection system was 2.63, which suggested that the MIP-coated CDs had better spectral sensitivity for the template molecules than the NIP-coated CDs. It dued to the fact that the recognition sites on the imprinted nanospheres had better steric matching ability for the template BPA molecules. The experimental assay displayed a linear relation between fluorescence intensity and the BPA concentrations in the range of 100nM to 4200nM. The detection limit was 30nM (S/N =3) (Fig. 5a). Compared with the reported methods [32–34], the developed approach possesses more reasonable linear range for BPA detection and lower detection limit (Table 1 ).

Furthermore, to investigate the analytical characteristics of proposed fluorescent method, the reproducibility and stability of the MIP-coated CDs sensor were tested. The results suggested that the reproducibility of the sensor is good. The sensor exists stably for at least one month at room temperature. Besides, the regerability assay of the sensor was also carried out. Fig. 5b reveals that the MIP-coated CDs sensor has the good regerability, which indicated that MIP-coated CDs was an excellent platform for the determination of BPA.

In order to test the selectivity of our proposed MIP-coated CDs method, various potential substances including phenol, o-nitrophenol (o-NP) and paranitrophenol (4-NP), and some interfering ions, such as K+, Na+, Ca2+, Zn2+, CO3 2−, SO4 2−, Fe3+, Ag+, Pb2+, Hg2+ and Cu2+ were measured. As displayed in Fig. 6 , the potential coexistent substances did not interfere with the determination of BPA by our presented MIP-coated CDs approach. Besides, the selectivity assays by NIP-coated CDs were also carried out (Fig. S3, Supplementary material). These results further demonstrated that MIP-coated CDs nanocomposites could be applied to selectively detect BPA molecule in an aqueous solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of BPA in real river water samples
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the applicability, the proposed approach was used for the detection of BPA in river water samples. The detected BPA content in river samples were derived from the standard curve and the regression equation. Moreover, the average recovery test was performed by using the standard addition method. Meanwhile, the traditional HPLC assay for the detection of BPA also was measured with the identical sample pretreatments procedures. The results are listed in Table 2 . The data indicate that the results by the present MIP coated CDs based sensor correlate well with those from HPLC method, which further suggested that the new developed approach had the potentiality of determining BPA in real samples.

In summary, a label-free fluorescent approach for detecting bisphenol A was successfully developed based on MIP-coated CDs nanocomposite. The MIP matrix coated carbon dots were fabricated by in situ hydrothermal synthesis. The new fluorescent biosensor incorporates the fluorescent property of CDs and high selectivity of MIP. Moreover, the present method exhibits the potential applications in the field of environmental monitoring and evaluation.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the Natural Science Foundation of China (21575123 and 21501146), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20150424 and BK20140464), the Opening Foundation of the Key Laboratory for Preparation and Application of Ordered Structural Materials of Guangdong Province (KLPAOSM201507), and the Industry-University-Research Cooperative Innovation Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BY2014108-19).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2016.01.010.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances in molecular imprinting technology: current status, challenges and highlighted applications
Molecularly imprinted polymers and their use in biomimetic sensors
Molecular imprinting under molecular crowding conditions: an aid to the synthesis of a high-capacity polymeric sorbent for triazine herbicides
Template recognition in inorganic–organic hybrid films prepared by the sol–gel process
Preparation and properties of fluorescein-labelled dextrans
NTP-CERHR expert panel report on the reproductive and developmental toxicity of bisphenol A
Determination of free and total bisphenol A in human urine to assess daily uptake as a basis for a valid risk assessment
Potential of supramolecular solvents for the extraction of contaminants in liquid foods
An extensive new literature concerning low-dose effects of bisphenol A shows the need for a new risk assessment
Ultrasensitive one-step rapid visual detection of bisphenol A in water samples by label-free aptasensor
Molecularly imprinted polymers for solid phase extraction

Dispersive liquid–liquid microextraction combined with high-performance liquid chromatography-UV detection as a very simple, rapid and sensitive method for the determination of bisphenol A in water samples
Sensitivity and selectivity determination of bisphenol A using SWCNT-CD conjugate modified glassy carbon electrode
Determination of bisphenol A using a flow injection inhibitory chemiluminescence method
Determination of bisphenol A in water via inhibition of silver nanoparticles-enhanced chemiluminescence
Composite of CdTe quantum dots and molecularly imprinted polymer as a sensing material for cytochrome C
A novel composite of graphene quantum dots and molecularly imprinted polymer for fluorescent detection of paranitrophenol
An overview of nanoparticles commonly used in fluorescent bioimaging
Glowing graphene quantum dots and carbon dots: properties, syntheses, and biological applications
Carbon quantum dots and their applications
Self-assembly of TGA-capped CdTe nanocrystals into three-dimensional luminescent nanostructures
Preparation of fluorescent SiO2 particles with single CdTe nanocrystal cores by the reverse microemulsion method
Efficient one-pot synthesis of molecularly imprinted silica nanospheres embedded carbon dots for fluorescent dopamine optosensing
Molecularly imprinted silica nanospheres embedded CdSe quantum dots for highly selective and sensitive optosensing of pyrethroids
A novel sol–gel–material prepared by a surface imprinting technique for the selective solid-phase extraction of bisphenol A
Luminescent CdS quantum dots as selective ion probe
Surface molecular imprinting on Mn-doped ZnS quantum dots for room-temperature phosphorescence optosensing of pentachlorophenol in water
Tyrosinase-modified boron-doped diamond electrodes for the determination of phenol derivatives
Laccase-catalyzed removal of bisphenol-A from water: protective effect of PEG on enzyme activity
Electrochemical sensor for bisphenol A detection based on molecularly imprinted polymers and gold nanoparticles

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Guoliang Liu received his BS degree in biology from Henan Normal University (China) in 2004, MS degree in Biochemistry and Molecular Biology from Shantou University (China) in 2009, and PhD degree in Biochemistry and Molecular Biology from Jinan University (China) in 2013. Now he is working at the Yancheng Institute of Technology (China) as a Lecturer. His research interests are in the areas of the synthesis of novel nanomaterials and their application, and biosensor.

Zhuo Chen received her BS degree from Yancheng Institute of Technology (China) in 2012, and then she entered the Master course in Auhui University of Science and Technology (China) in the same year, majored in Applied Chemistry. Now, she is engaged in the construction and application of biosensor.

Xiaoying Jiang received her BS degree from Yancheng Institute of Technology (China) in 2013, and then she entered the Master course in Changzhou University (China) in the same year, majored in Applied Chemistry. Now, she is engaged in the construction and application of biosensor.

Da-Qian Feng received her BS degree in chemistry from Hubei University for Nationalities (China) in 2006 and MS degree from Shantou University in 2009. In 2012, she received her PhD degree in biochemistry and molecular biology from Jinan University. Now she is working at the Yancheng Institute of Technology (China) as a Lecturer. Her research interests are in the areas of bioanalytical chemistry, nanobiosensors and coordination chemistry.
Junyu Zhao is an undergaduate in Yancheng Institute of Technology (China). Now, he is engaged in the construction and application of biosensor.
Dahe Fan is a professor of Yancheng Institute of Technology (China). His research interests are in the areas of analytical chemistry and biosensor.

Wei Wang received his BS and PhD degrees in analytical chemistry from Nanjing University (China) in 1991 and 2007, respectively. Now he is a professor of Yancheng Institute of Technology (China). His research interests are in the areas of microfluidics and electrochemical detection.
